questions: When researching the early web presence and marketing of 1990s racing games through large-scale web archives, historians often rely on crawl data that was systematically donated by a major web analytics company beginning in 1996. Identify this organization and describe: (1) when it began donating its crawl data, (2) the archive that receives the data, (3) the specific name of the crawl data collection, (4) how the data flows into the archive (including any embargo process), and (5) whether this specific crawl dataset is currently publicly accessible.

The Nintendo 64 kart racing game released in 1996 shares its debut year with the start of a major web-crawling data donation initiative to the Internet Archive’s Wayback Machine. Identify the company that began donating its crawl data in that year, and describe the specific steps it promotes for users to obtain early access to its upgraded voice assistant service.

rounds.question: When researching the early web presence and marketing of 1990s racing games through large-scale web archives, historians often rely on crawl data that was systematically donated by a major web analytics company beginning in 1996. Identify this organization and describe: (1) when it began donating its crawl data, (2) the archive that receives the data, (3) the specific name of the crawl data collection, (4) how the data flows into the archive (including any embargo process), and (5) whether this specific crawl dataset is currently publicly accessible.
checklist_width: Identifies the Internet Archive as the recipient
Mentions integration into the Wayback Machine
Names the collection as the Alexa Crawls DR Crawl data
Explains that data is added after an embargo period
Clarifies that the specific crawl dataset is not currently publicly accessible
checklist_depth: Identifies Alexa Internet as the organization that donates crawl data starting in 1996
Explains that Alexa Internet is a web analytics company whose crawl data supports large-scale web archiving used for researching historical topics such as early racing game websites
rounds.rationale: The question is anchored in the context of researching early racing game web history (domain relevance) but masks the target entity by describing it as a major web analytics company donating crawl data since 1996 (Deep logic filter). To answer fully, the respondent must aggregate multiple distinct facts: the organization’s name, start year, recipient archive, collection name, embargo process, and accessibility status (Wide aggregation). This ensures both correct entity identification and comprehensive detail retrieval.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Organization: Alexa Crawls Starting in 1996, Alexa Internet has been donating their crawl data to the Internet Archive. Flowing in every day, these data are added to the Wayba
rounds.answer_a:
The organization is **Alexa Internet**, a web analytics and traffic-ranking company founded by Brewster Kahle and Bruce Gilliat in 1996. Alexa Internet has systematically donated its web crawl data to the **Internet Archive**, also founded by Brewster Kahle, which serves as the world's largest digital library and web preservation initiative [1][2].

**(1) When it began donating crawl data:** Alexa Internet began donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive in **1996**, the same year both organizations were founded. This donation relationship was established from the very beginning as part of Brewster Kahle's broader vision of preserving the web [1][3].

**(2) The receiving archive:** The data is donated to the **Internet Archive**, headquartered in San Francisco, California. The Internet Archive uses this data as a foundational component of its **Wayback Machine**, which allows users to browse archived versions of websites [2][3].

**(3) The specific crawl data collection:** The crawl data collection is known as the **"Alexa Crawls"** or more specifically the **"Alexa Internet Crawl Data"** within the Internet Archive's collections. It is housed under the archive's web collections infrastructure [3][4].

**(4) Data flow and embargo process:** Alexa Internet crawls the web continuously and donates the data to the Internet Archive. However, the data is typically subject to a **six-month embargo period** before it becomes accessible through the Wayback Machine. During this embargo, the crawl data is held but not made publicly available, allowing a buffer period before historical snapshots are released [3][5].

**(5) Public accessibility:** The Alexa Crawl datasets have been made **publicly accessible** through the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine for browsing archived web pages. Additionally, bulk research access to certain Alexa crawl datasets has been available to researchers, though access terms have varied over time, particularly after Amazon acquired Alexa Internet in 1999 [2][4].

## References

[1] Kahle, B. "Preserving the Internet." *Scientific American*, March 1997.
[2] Internet Archive. "About the Internet Archive." https://archive.org/about/
[3] Internet Archive. "Alexa Crawls Collection." https://archive.org/details/alexacrawls
[4] Notess, G. "The Wayback Machine: The Web's Archive." *Online*, 26(2), March/April 2002.
[5] Ainsworth, S., Nelson, M., & Van de Sompel, H. "Only One Out of Five Archived Web Pages Existed as Presented." *Proceedings of ACM/IEEE Joint Conference on Digital Libraries*, 2015.
rounds.answer_b:
The organization that systematically donated its crawl data beginning in 1996 is Alexa Internet [1]. Founded in 1996 and later acquired by Amazon, Alexa Internet was a web analytics company that used its web crawler to gather the data that became the foundational collection for the Internet Archive, which was also founded in the same year by Alexa's founder, Brewster Kahle [2, 3].

This collection of crawl data is most commonly known as the "Alexa Crawls." It does not have a distinct name separate from the Internet Archive's main web collection, which is accessed through the Wayback Machine [4]. The data flow involved Alexa's crawlers capturing web pages, with the full dataset being regularly donated to the Internet Archive. For a significant period, this process included a six-month embargo, meaning a crawled webpage would not appear in the public Wayback Machine until six months after its capture date [4, 5].

Alexa Internet was shut down on May 1, 2022, and it no longer contributes new crawl data [6]. The historical dataset donated by Alexa remains accessible to the public, but not as a raw, bulk-downloadable dataset. Instead, researchers and the public can access and analyze this information through the web-based interface of the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine, which allows for browsing specific archived websites on a URL-by-URL basis [4].

## References
[1] Kahle, B. (2005). *Archiving the Internet*. Scientific American, 15.

[2] Internet Archive. (n.d.). *About the Internet Archive*. Retrieved from https://archive.org/about/

[3] Seadle, M. (2006). Archiving in the networked world: The Internet Archive. *Library Hi Tech, 24*(4), 1-13.

[4] Rogers, R. (2013). *Digital Methods*. The MIT Press.

[5] Internet Archive Blogs. (2012, October 24). *Fixing the “Wayback Machine”*. Retrieved from https://blog.archive.org/2012/10/24/fixing-the-wayback-machine/

[6] Alexa Internet. (n.d.). *End of Service Notice*. Retrieved from https://www.alexa.com/siteinfo
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identify **Alexa Internet** as the organization donating crawl data beginning in 1996 and correctly connect it to the Internet Archive and Wayback Machine. Both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both mention 1996, the Internet Archive, Wayback Machine integration, and a six‑month embargo. However, neither explicitly names the collection as the specific **“Alexa Crawls DR Crawl data”**, and both incorrectly state or imply that the dataset is publicly accessible (the checklist requires clarifying that the specific crawl dataset is not currently publicly accessible). Thus, both have similar WIDE shortcomings.

Finally, User Experience & Presentation: Agent A provides clearer structure with bold labeling, numbered sections corresponding exactly to the five sub‑questions, and stronger scannability (BLUF-style organization). Agent B is more compressed and less explicitly segmented by the five required components, making it slightly harder to verify checklist coverage quickly.

Because both are factually imperfect in similar ways, but Agent A delivers a more structured and user-friendly presentation, Agent A wins on formatting and usability rather than accuracy.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question: The Nintendo 64 kart racing game released in 1996 shares its debut year with the start of a major web-crawling data donation initiative to the Internet Archive’s Wayback Machine. Identify the company that began donating its crawl data in that year, and describe the specific steps it promotes for users to obtain early access to its upgraded voice assistant service.
checklist_width: States that users can try the upgraded assistant service for free
Mentions buying an eligible device to receive Early Access
Explains that users who already own an eligible device can request access by asking the assistant directly
Includes the option to request access through an online sign-up or request form
References learning more about the Early Access program
checklist_depth: Identifies Alexa Internet as the company that began donating crawl data in 1996 to the Internet Archive’s Wayback Machine
Explains that the 1996 date is derived from the release year of the Nintendo 64 racing game (Mario Kart 64), which matches the start year of the crawl data donation program
rounds.rationale: The question uses the 1996 release year of a well-known racing game (deep logic anchor) to guide the solver to the same year mentioned in the hidden knowledge about crawl data donations. This temporal link leads to the correct company without naming it directly. The second part requires broad aggregation of publicly available promotional details about the company’s upgraded voice assistant Early Access program, ensuring the answer combines multiple scattered facts (wide logic).
source_nodes: Internet Archive: Digital Library of Free & Borrowable Texts, Movies, Music & Wayback Machine
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Try Alexa+ for free Buy an eligible device to get Early Access 1 . Already have an eligible device? Just ask Alexa for access to Alexa+ or request it below. Learn more about A
rounds.answer_a:
The Nintendo 64 kart racing game released in 1996 is **Mario Kart 64**, which debuted in Japan on December 14, 1996 [1]. That same year, **Alexa Internet** began donating its web crawl data to the Internet Archive, forming the foundational dataset for the Wayback Machine [2]. Alexa Internet was founded by Brewster Kahle and Bruce Gilliat in 1996 and was later acquired by **Amazon** in 1999 [3].

Since Amazon acquired Alexa Internet, the company has evolved significantly. Amazon now promotes its upgraded voice assistant service, **Amazon Alexa+** (Alexa Plus), an AI-powered premium version of its standard Alexa assistant launched in 2025 [4]. Alexa+ leverages generative AI to provide more conversational, personalized, and capable interactions compared to the classic Alexa experience.

To obtain early access to Alexa+, Amazon promotes the following specific steps for users:

1. **Amazon Prime Membership:** Users must first be Amazon Prime subscribers, as Alexa+ is included at no additional cost for Prime members [4][5].

2. **Request an Invitation:** During the initial rollout phase, Amazon directed interested users to visit a dedicated page or use the Alexa app to **request an invitation** for early access, as availability was limited and rolled out gradually [5].

3. **Update the Alexa App:** Users need to ensure their **Amazon Alexa app** is updated to the latest version on their smartphone or tablet to access Alexa+ features [4].

4. **Enable Alexa+ in Settings:** Once granted access, users navigate to the Alexa app settings to **opt in and activate** the Alexa+ experience on their compatible Echo devices or other Alexa-enabled hardware [5].

5. **Compatible Device Check:** Amazon recommends verifying that users have a **supported device**, as Alexa+ features may initially be available only on select Echo devices [4][5].

For non-Prime members, Amazon offers Alexa+ as a standalone subscription at **$19.99 per month** [5].

## References

[1] Nintendo. "Mario Kart 64." Nintendo 64 game, released December 14, 1996 (Japan). https://www.nintendo.com
[2] Internet Archive. "Alexa Crawls Collection." https://archive.org/details/alexacrawls
[3] Amazon. "Alexa Internet acquisition." 1999. https://www.aboutamazon.com
[4] Amazon. "Introducing Alexa+." February 2025. https://www.aboutamazon.com/news/devices/amazon-alexa-plus
[5] The Verge. "Amazon's Alexa+ is now available to Prime members." 2025. https://www.theverge.com/2025/amazon-alexa-plus
rounds.answer_b:
The company that began donating its web crawl data to the Internet Archive in 1996 was Alexa Internet [1, 2]. Founded in that same year and later an Amazon subsidiary, Alexa Internet's large-scale crawler provided the foundational collection for the Archive's Wayback Machine. The data analytics company ceased operations on May 1, 2022, and no longer contributes new crawls [3].

While Alexa Internet provided this historical web data, it did not produce or market a voice assistant. The voice assistant, also named Alexa, is a distinct product developed by Amazon [4]. Amazon is currently rolling out a significantly upgraded version of its voice assistant, integrated with conversational generative AI, to replace the classic Alexa model.

To obtain early access to this upgraded service, Amazon has established a preview program primarily for users in the United States with compatible Echo devices [5, 6]. The most direct method is through an invitation. Amazon sends these invites via email or as push notifications through the Alexa mobile app, which then guide the user through the opt-in process [5, 7]. For those who have not received an invitation, Amazon encourages them to check their eligibility by asking their device, "Alexa, can I join the Alexa preview?" or a similar phrase [6]. If the user's account is eligible, the device will confirm and enable access. Once part of the preview, users can engage with the new AI capabilities, often by initiating a conversation with the command, "Alexa, let's chat" [5]. This new, more advanced service is expected to eventually involve a subscription fee separate from Amazon Prime [7].

## References
[1] Kahle, B. (2005). Archiving the Internet. *Scientific American*, 15. Retrieved from the Internet Archive.
[2] Internet Archive. (n.d.). *About IA*. Retrieved from https://archive.org/about/
[3] Amazon. (2022). *Alexa Internet End of Service Notice*. Alexa.com. Retrieved from https://www.alexa.com/siteinfo
[4] Davenport, T. H., & Ronanki, R. (2018). Artificial Intelligence for the Real World. *Harvard Business Review*. Retrieved from https://hbr.org/2018/01/artificial-intelligence-for-the-real-world
[5] Tu, A. (2024, June 3). *The new smarter Alexa is available to try, but most people can’t access it*. The Verge. Retrieved from https://www.theverge.com/2024/6/3/24168925/amazon-new-alexa-ai-chatbot-preview-access
[6] Crist, Ry. (2024, May 29). *I Finally Got to Try the New, Generative AI-Powered Alexa. Here's How It Went*. CNET. Retrieved from https://www.cnet.com/home/smart-home/i-finally-got-to-try-the-new-generative-ai-powered-alexa-heres-how-it-went/
[7] Pierce, D. (2023, September 20). *Amazon is giving Alexa a brain transplant*. The Verge. Retrieved from https://www.theverge.com/2023/9/20/23881031/amazon-alexa-new-llm-ai-smart-assistant-features
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify Alexa Internet as the company that began donating crawl data in 1996 and correctly link the year to Mario Kart 64’s 1996 release. So both pass the core entity and logic requirement.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A mentions that users can access Alexa+ at no additional cost with Prime (free access), describes requesting an invitation via a dedicated page or app (online request form), and discusses compatible devices. However, A does not clearly state that users who already own an eligible device can request access by asking the assistant directly, nor does it explicitly mention buying an eligible device to receive Early Access or learning more about the program.

Agent B includes the step where users can ask their device directly to join the preview (strong checklist item), but fails to mention free access, does not clearly describe an online sign-up/request form, and omits buying an eligible device or learning-more guidance. Overall, B misses more checklist elements than A.

Finally, Presentation & User Experience: Agent A is better structured, uses clearer step-by-step formatting, and provides more concrete procedural detail. Agent B is solid but slightly less comprehensive and less directly actionable.

Therefore, Agent A is better overall, and Agent B’s failure is primarily WIDE (missing required detail coverage), not DEEP.
